content:
Is it intentional that Box::try_new() doesn't return an Err(ENOMEM) on failure?

I just wanted to return it as a Result, but apparently I'm forced to cast it with .into()

the error that Box::try_new returns is AllocError. this means that if it fails, it must be becausr there is no memory left. the general Error type (that declares the constant ENOMEM) also can represent othrr failures such as EINVAL.
so yes, it is intentional. it also allows for better optimizations, since Result<Box<T>, AllocError> is pointer sized.
"Optimizations" in sense of compiler / performance optimizations?

The question mark operator automatically converts error types for you, so if you do Box::try_new()? in a function that returns the usual kernel error type, then it will work and result in an ENOMEM error.
So if the box is the last thing you return, then one thing you can do is return Ok(Box::try_new(foo)?).

Nope, that only works if you're not dealing with C abstractions where you have to clean up stuff on error with unsafe.
Lacking goto, you then need to match for the Error, clean up, convert the AllocError, then return
Ah, I understand what you're doing now. Yeah in that case an explicit conversion is the easiest way to go about it.

Philipp Stanner said:

"Optimizations" in sense of compiler / performance optimizations?

yes, Result<Box<T>, AllocError> fits in 64 bits, Result<Box<T>, Error> needs 128 bits, so it cannot be returned in just rax.

Philipp Stanner said:

Nope, that only works if you're not dealing with C abstractions where you have to clean up stuff on error with unsafe.
Lacking goto, you then need to match for the Error, clean up, convert the AllocError, then return

you can create another rust function that returns a result and in the C function you call that

Another way to go about it is this:

let b = match Box::try_new(foo) {
    Ok(b) => b,
    Err(AllocError) => {
        clean_up();
        return Err(ENOMEM);
    },
};

By typing Err(AllocError), you are asserting that Box::try_new returns an AllocError, and the code will not compile if there are other possible reasons Box::try_new could fail.

I've sometimes seen comments along the lines of "// this function can only fail with ESRCH", and this pattern allows you to encode that assertion as a checked condition in your code. As a similar example, see BadFdError from my file patchset. By using the same syntax, you can assert in your code that an fget call can only fail with EBADF and that no other error conditions exist for that method. I think that is useful.

Benno Lossin said:

Philipp Stanner said:

Nope, that only works if you're not dealing with C abstractions where you have to clean up stuff on error with unsafe.
Lacking goto, you then need to match for the Error, clean up, convert the AllocError, then return

you can create another rust function that returns a result and in the C function you call that

That can be a workaround that helps a bit, yes, but only if the unwind path is always the same. Otherwise you'd need several helper functions.
It's all solvable, obviously.
Is it possible in your use case to use scope guard for cleanup?

Gary Guo said:

Is it possible in your use case to use scope guard for cleanup?

You mean defining a separate scope with { }?
Probably not, we have a type named ScopeGuard that can help you release resources (it provides the same mechanism as go's defer keyword, but you can also "cancel" it).

goto on cocaine then ;p
Danilo (and I) will provde some patches soon, then we could look at that in-line
One small clarification here: go's defer runs when the function exits, while ScopeGuard runs when it goes out of scope, so they're slightly different
